title: Why Evaluate Regression Differently Than Classification
concept: Regression models predict numbers, not labels, so accuracy and precision don't apply — evaluation instead measures the distance between each prediction and the true value.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In classification, what does accuracy measure?

options.text: The percentage of predictions that exactly match the correct label
The average distance between a prediction and the correct label
The squared size of the model's errors

exercises.explain: Right — accuracy counts exact matches. That works great for labels like "spam" or "not spam." But what happens when there's no exact label to match?

cards.title: Was $351,200 "correct"?
cards.body: Your regression model predicts a house is worth $351,200. The actual sale price was $350,000. Is that right or wrong? There's no clean yes/no answer — it's close, but not exact.

cards.title: What does "evaluating regression" mean?
cards.body: Evaluating a regression model means measuring how far off its predictions are from the true values, on average — not whether each prediction is exactly right.

cards.title: Archery, not a checklist
cards.body: Classification grading is like a checklist: did you circle the correct box, yes or no? Regression grading is like archery: every arrow (prediction) lands somewhere near the bullseye (the true value), and you measure the distance.

cards.title: Off by 1.3 degrees
cards.body: A weather model predicts 72.3°F and the actual temperature is 71°F. It isn't "wrong" the way a mislabeled photo is wrong — it's off by 1.3 degrees, a small distance that still counts as useful.

cards.title: "% accurate" doesn't apply here
cards.body: You'll hear people say a regression model is "90% accurate," but accuracy is a classification concept built for counting exact matches. Regression needs its own metrics built for measuring distance, which this unit covers.

cards.title: Classification metrics vs. regression metrics
cards.body: Different outputs need different report cards:

left.text:
Accuracy, Precision, Recall
Counts of right vs. wrong labels

right.text:
RMSE, MAE, R²
Distance between prediction and truth

exercises.prompt: Why can't you use "accuracy" to evaluate a regression model that predicts exact prices?

options.text: Because predictions almost never exactly match the true price, even when they're very close and useful
Because regression models don't make predictions
Because accuracy can only be calculated for text data

exercises.explain: Exactly. A prediction of $349,900 vs an actual $350,000 would count as "wrong" under strict accuracy, even though it's an excellent prediction.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Regression evaluation metrics measure the distance between predicted and actual values, not whether they match exactly.
exercises.explain: True! Distance-based metrics like RMSE and MAE capture how close a prediction is, not just whether it's a perfect match.

exercises.sentence: Classification metrics count exact label matches; regression metrics measure the ___ between predictions and true values.
exercises.explain: Regression metrics like RMSE and MAE quantify the distance, or error, between each prediction and the actual value.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a valid way to evaluate a regression model that predicts delivery time in minutes?

options.text: Calculate the average number of minutes each prediction was off from the actual delivery time
Calculate the percentage of predictions that landed on the exact same minute as the actual delivery time
Count how many predictions were labeled "fast" vs "slow"

exercises.explain: Averaging the error in minutes captures how close predictions typically are — exactly what distance-based regression metrics do.

exercises.prompt: A hospital builds a model to predict a patient's recovery time in days. A colleague suggests grading it with "accuracy: percent of predictions that exactly match the true recovery day." What's the better approach, and why?

options.text: Use a distance-based metric like average error in days, since being off by 1 day is very different from being off by 20 days, and exact accuracy treats both as equally "wrong"
Keep the accuracy metric, since medical predictions must always be exact
Round every prediction to the nearest week so accuracy becomes easier to achieve
Accuracy is fine because recovery time is technically a category, not a number

exercises.explain: Exactly! A distance-based metric rewards "off by 1 day" far more than "off by 20 days," which is what actually matters for a continuous outcome. Next: the first of these distance-based metrics — RMSE.
